does: This paper compares two sparse autoencoder architectures—Crosscoders and Differential SAEs—for detecting backdoor attacks in language models through mechanistic interpretability. Using a controlled SQL injection backdoor triggered by year-based context on SmolLM2-360M, the authors systematically evaluate both approaches across multiple layers and fine-tuning methods. The core finding is that Diff-SAE substantially outperforms Crosscoders for isolating backdoor-related features, achieving a Backdoor Isolation Score of 0.40 with perfect precision and zero false positives, while Crosscoders fail to isolate backdoors (BIS below 0.02). The evaluation covers both LoRA and full-rank fine-tuning regimes across transformer layers 14, 18, 22, and 26, demonstrating the robustness and reliability of Diff-SAE for this mechanistic interpretability task.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on mechanistic interpretability for AI safety, building on sparse autoencoder methods developed for understanding neural network feature representations. It responds to the open challenge of detecting backdoor attacks—a critical problem in adversarial robustness and model security that has received attention from both the interpretability and adversarial examples communities. The paper builds directly on prior work developing Crosscoders for comparative analysis and Differential SAEs as a novel architecture, advancing techniques for isolating causal features in model activations. It connects to broader research on fine-tuning vulnerabilities, prompt injection attacks, and the interpretability of model behavior under distribution shift or adversarial conditioning.
enables: This work establishes Diff-SAE as a practical tool for automated backdoor detection in language models, enabling safer deployment and auditing of fine-tuned systems. By demonstrating reliable feature isolation across layers and training regimes, it opens pathways for scaling mechanistic approaches to detect more sophisticated backdoors and adversarial modifications. The systematic comparison provides a foundation for developing hybrid or improved architectures that combine strengths of different SAE variants. Future work can leverage these findings to create automated defenses, verify model integrity before deployment, and understand how backdoors distribute across model layers—potentially enabling removal or mitigation strategies that preserve model capability.
